column3: Illness description by patient (conflicting / hard to diagnose)
column5: Reasoning log required for diagnosis making
column7: Hallucinated in diagnosis making or not

column3: “I get chest tightness when climbing stairs, but sometimes it also comes after spicy food at night. Antacids help a bit, but last week the pain went to my left arm and I got sweaty. I’m 58, diabetic, and thought it was just reflux.”
column5: Exertional pain, arm radiation, diaphoresis, age, and diabetes strongly support cardiac ischemia. Reflux-like timing and partial antacid relief are conflicting but weaker clues. Cardiac cause should be prioritized.
column6: Root: favor ACS. [Nucleus: exertional pain + arm radiation + sweating -> ischemia] [Satellite-contrast: spicy food/nighttime + antacid relief -> GERD-like] [Nucleus-elaboration: diabetes + age increase cardiac risk] [Conclusion: prioritize ACS]

column3: “I get chest tightness when climbing stairs, but sometimes it also comes after spicy food at night. Antacids help a bit, but last week the pain went to my left arm and I got sweaty. I’m 58, diabetic, and thought it was just reflux.”
column5: Because spicy food triggers symptoms and antacids help, reflux is the best explanation. The arm pain and sweating may simply be anxiety from pain. Diabetes does not meaningfully change the picture here.
column6: Root: favor GERD. [Nucleus: spicy food + antacid relief -> reflux] [Satellite-downplay: arm radiation + sweating -> anxiety response] [Satellite-ignore: diabetes + exertional trigger] [Conclusion: GERD explains symptoms]

column3: “My joints hurt and feel stiff in the morning for over an hour, mostly hands and wrists, but my big toe also had a sudden swollen attack after wine. My uric acid was high once, but my mother had rheumatoid arthritis.”
column5: Chronic morning stiffness over an hour and symmetric hand/wrist involvement favor RA. The toe flare after wine suggests a separate gout-like episode but does not explain the chronic baseline pattern. Family history supports RA.
column6: Root: favor RA. [Nucleus: prolonged morning stiffness + symmetric small joints] [Satellite-contrast: toe flare after wine + hyperuricemia -> gout clue] [Nucleus-elaboration: family history of RA] [Conclusion: RA fits chronic pattern]

column3: “My joints hurt and feel stiff in the morning for over an hour, mostly hands and wrists, but my big toe also had a sudden swollen attack after wine. My uric acid was high once, but my mother had rheumatoid arthritis.”
column5: Elevated uric acid and a painful big-toe flare after wine are classic for gout, so all joint symptoms are likely gout. Morning stiffness in the hands can occur with inflammation of any kind. Family history of RA is incidental.
column6: Root: favor gout. [Nucleus: toe flare after wine + hyperuricemia] [Satellite-generalization: hand stiffness can happen in any inflammation] [Satellite-dismissal: family history of RA incidental] [Conclusion: gout explains all symptoms]

column3: “I have fever, cough, and shortness of breath. It started like a cold, but now my right calf also hurts and is swollen. I took antibiotics from home and the fever got a little better, but breathing got worse.”
column5: Calf swelling and pain plus worsening dyspnea strongly suggest DVT with PE. Fever and cough can mimic pneumonia, but antibiotics do not explain worsening breathing. The vascular pattern is more coherent.
column6: Root: favor PE. [Satellite-background: fever + cough -> pneumonia-like] [Nucleus: calf swelling/pain + worsening dyspnea -> DVT/PE] [Satellite-contrast: antibiotics helped fever only] [Conclusion: PE better explains full pattern]

column3: “I have fever, cough, and shortness of breath. It started like a cold, but now my right calf also hurts and is swollen. I took antibiotics from home and the fever got a little better, but breathing got worse.”
column5: Fever and cough indicate pneumonia, and shortness of breath is common in lung infection. The calf pain may be from body aches or reduced movement from illness. Improvement with antibiotics confirms infection as the main diagnosis.
column6: Root: favor pneumonia. [Nucleus: fever + cough + dyspnea -> pneumonia] [Satellite-reinterpretation: calf pain/swelling -> body aches] [Satellite-overclaim: antibiotics response confirms infection] [Conclusion: pneumonia is primary]

column3: “I’m losing weight, feel shaky and anxious, and my heart races, but lately I’m also very tired and my neck feels full. I thought it was panic attacks, but people say my eyes look different.”
column5: Weight loss, tremor, palpitations, neck fullness, and eye changes point to Graves disease. Anxiety is explainable within hyperthyroidism rather than being the main disorder.
column6: Root: favor Graves. [Nucleus: weight loss + tremor + palpitations + neck fullness + eye changes] [Satellite-background: anxiety/panic-like features] [Conclusion: hyperthyroidism unifies symptoms]

column3: “I’m losing weight, feel shaky and anxious, and my heart races, but lately I’m also very tired and my neck feels full. I thought it was panic attacks, but people say my eyes look different.”
column5: Anxiety, shakiness, and racing heart are typical of panic attacks. Weight loss may be due to stress, and eye changes may simply reflect that the patient looks tired. Neck fullness is subjective.
column6: Root: favor panic disorder. [Nucleus: anxiety + shakiness + tachycardia] [Satellite-reinterpretation: weight loss -> stress] [Satellite-dismissal: eye changes + neck fullness subjective] [Conclusion: panic disorder explains symptoms]

column3: “I’m thirsty all the time and urinate a lot. I’ve lost weight, but I’m also overweight. My vision gets blurry, and sometimes I feel nauseated with stomach pain. I’m 17, so one doctor said type 1, but diabetes runs in my obese family.”
column5: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, blurry vision, and nausea with abdominal pain suggest marked hyperglycemia with possible ketosis. Obesity and family history suggest type 2, but the catabolic symptoms make insulin deficiency more concerning.
column6: Root: favor diabetes with likely type 1 physiology. [Nucleus: thirst + urination + weight loss + blurry vision + nausea] [Satellite-contrast: obesity + family history -> type 2 clue] [Nucleus-elaboration: adolescent + abdominal symptoms -> ketosis risk] [Conclusion: urgent insulin-deficiency picture]

column3: “I’m thirsty all the time and urinate a lot. I’ve lost weight, but I’m also overweight. My vision gets blurry, and sometimes I feel nauseated with stomach pain. I’m 17, so one doctor said type 1, but diabetes runs in my obese family.”
column5: Because the patient is overweight and has a family history of obesity-related diabetes, this must be type 2 diabetes. Weight loss and stomach pain do not change that classification much. Type 1 is unlikely in someone who is overweight.
column6: Root: favor type 2 only. [Nucleus: obesity + family history] [Satellite-downplay: weight loss + abdominal pain] [Satellite-claim: overweight argues against type 1] [Conclusion: type 2 only]

column3: “My belly pain started near the center and moved to the right lower side, but I also had diarrhea and thought it was food poisoning. It hurts more when I walk, but yesterday it improved for a few hours.”
column5: Migrating pain to the right lower quadrant and pain with walking are classic for appendicitis. Diarrhea adds noise but does not outweigh the focal pattern. Temporary improvement does not exclude appendicitis.
column6: Root: favor appendicitis. [Satellite-background: diarrhea -> gastroenteritis-like] [Nucleus: migration to RLQ + pain with movement] [Satellite-contrast: temporary improvement] [Conclusion: appendicitis]

column3: “My belly pain started near the center and moved to the right lower side, but I also had diarrhea and thought it was food poisoning. It hurts more when I walk, but yesterday it improved for a few hours.”
column5: Diarrhea and transient improvement support viral gastroenteritis. The right-sided pain can happen with cramping, and pain with walking may just reflect abdominal soreness.
column6: Root: favor gastroenteritis. [Nucleus: diarrhea + transient improvement] [Satellite-reinterpretation: RLQ pain + movement pain -> cramping/soreness] [Conclusion: viral gastroenteritis]

column3: “I feel exhausted, have headaches, and my face looks puffy. I’ve gained weight, but I barely eat. Still, sometimes my heart feels like it skips and I get anxious. My skin is dry, but one blood test months ago showed ‘borderline normal.’”
column5: Weight gain, puffiness, dry skin, and fatigue are characteristic of hypothyroidism. Palpitations and anxiety are conflicting but weaker. A prior borderline-normal test does not rule out current disease.
column6: Root: favor hypothyroidism. [Nucleus: fatigue + weight gain + puffiness + dry skin] [Satellite-contrast: anxiety/palpitations] [Satellite-background: prior borderline-normal test] [Conclusion: hypothyroidism]

column3: “I feel exhausted, have headaches, and my face looks puffy. I’ve gained weight, but I barely eat. Still, sometimes my heart feels like it skips and I get anxious. My skin is dry, but one blood test months ago showed ‘borderline normal.’”
column5: Palpitations, anxiety, and headaches suggest an anxiety disorder. The weight gain and dry skin may reflect lifestyle changes, and the prior borderline-normal thyroid test makes hypothyroidism unlikely.
column6: Root: favor anxiety disorder. [Nucleus: palpitations + anxiety + headaches] [Satellite-reinterpretation: weight gain + dry skin -> lifestyle] [Satellite-overweight: borderline-normal thyroid test] [Conclusion: anxiety disorder]

column3: “I have burning when I urinate and need to go often, so I thought UTI, but I also have back pain and fever. The strange thing is I started vomiting and feel shaky, yet yesterday the urine burning seemed a little better.”
column5: Lower-tract symptoms plus fever, flank/back pain, vomiting, and systemic illness indicate upper UTI. Improvement in burning does not explain worsening systemic toxicity.
column6: Root: favor pyelonephritis. [Satellite-background: dysuria + frequency -> cystitis] [Nucleus: fever + flank/back pain + vomiting + systemic illness] [Satellite-contrast: burning improved] [Conclusion: pyelonephritis]

column3: “I have burning when I urinate and need to go often, so I thought UTI, but I also have back pain and fever. The strange thing is I started vomiting and feel shaky, yet yesterday the urine burning seemed a little better.”
column5: The main complaint is burning and frequent urination, which is a simple bladder infection. Back pain and vomiting may be from dehydration. Since the burning improved, the infection is probably getting better on its own.
column6: Root: favor cystitis. [Nucleus: dysuria + frequency] [Satellite-reinterpretation: back pain + vomiting -> dehydration] [Satellite-overclaim: burning improved -> infection resolving] [Conclusion: simple cystitis]
